description: A V8 sandbox bypass vulnerability caused by a stack use-after-return (UAR) in JSAtomicsCondition::WaitFor(). A SyncWaiterQueueNode (this_waiter) is allocated on the stack and enqueued into the condition variable's doubly-linked waiter queue (stored as an ExternalPointer waiter_queue_head in JSSynchronizationPrimitive). The function assumes that either (a) the notifying thread dequeues the node or (b) the timeout path calls DequeueExplicit() to remove it before returning. Under adversarial in-sandbox corruption, an attacker can corrupt the condition variable's state to appear as if a notifying thread already dequeued the current thread, causing DequeueExplicit() to find no matching node and return 0 without removing this_waiter. After WaitFor() returns and the stack frame is freed, the dangling this_waiter pointer remains in the global waiter queue. Subsequent enqueue/dequeue operations then access the freed stack memory, enabling a stack use-after-return that can be weaponized into an arbitrary controlled address write (e.g., by spraying the stack with WebAssembly locals). The attacker reaches this vulnerable code path by first 'reviving' the --harmony-struct experimental builtins (AtomicsMutexLock, AtomicsConditionWait): since the Code objects for these builtins always exist even when the flag is disabled, an attacker can corrupt a SharedFunctionInfo (obtained via CreateDynamicFunction's compilation cache) to point to these builtins and set up a compatible FeedbackCell.dispatch_handle, causing JSFunctionBuilder to install the experimental builtin code on a function without the flag being enabled.

fixes.description: Upstream fix patch exported from commit 792c326e896020f74533e0d7b629b9a1b35e555a for fixed-image builds.
fixes.evidence_text: [sandbox] Don't allow to use disabled builtins, pt.2
